ExampleDanish: Det var en svær opgave, men han blev endda færdig før tid.
ExampleEnglish: It was a difficult task, but he even finished ahead of time.
ContextualTip: This word is used for emphasis, often to express surprise that something exceeds expectations or to intensify a statement. It can also mean "even so" or "yet" when placed at the end of a sentence to show contrast.

ExampleDanish: Motion er med til at fremme sundheden.
ExampleEnglish: Exercise helps to promote health.
ContextualTip: This verb is often used in formal or professional contexts when talking about advancing a cause, a process, or a development. It is distinct from the adverb "fremme," which describes being at a destination or in the front.

ExampleDanish: Jeg har ikke set ham siden i går.
ExampleEnglish: I haven't seen him since yesterday.
ContextualTip: This word is a Swiss Army knife. It can mean "since" (time), "ago" (as in to år siden / "two years ago"), or it can be the definite form of en side (the page/side). When used as "since," it can function as both a preposition and a conjunction.

ExampleDanish: Det er et rigtigt svar.
ExampleEnglish: That is a correct answer.
ContextualTip: Use rigtigt as an adjective to mean "correct" or "real." It is also very common as an adverb to mean "really" or "very" (e.g., det er rigtigt koldt / "it is really cold"). Note that the form changes to rigtig if it describes a common gender noun (en rigtig beslutning).

ExampleDanish: Vi havde planlagt en tur; det blev imidlertid dårligt vejr.
ExampleEnglish: We had planned a trip; however, it turned into bad weather.
ContextualTip: This is a more formal version of dog or men. It is rarely used at the beginning of a sentence in Danish; it usually sits after the verb or the subject, functioning as a "parenthetical" transition word.

ExampleDanish: Det regnede, men vi tog alligevel til stranden.
ExampleEnglish: It rained, but we went to the beach anyway.
ContextualTip: Use this when something happens despite a reason why it shouldn't. It usually appears after the verb in a main clause or at the very end of the sentence for emphasis.

English: on the other hand / however / in contrast
ExampleDanish: Min bror kan lide te; jeg vil derimod hellere have kaffe.
ExampleEnglish: My brother likes tea; I, on the other hand, would rather have coffee.
ContextualTip: Used to contrast two different groups or ideas.While tværtimod is used to contradict a statement (opposite), derimod is used to contrast two different things or people. Think of it as "whereas" or "by contrast."

ExampleDanish: Maden var god, og desuden var den billig.
ExampleEnglish: The food was good, and besides, it was cheap.
ContextualTip: Use this when you want to add an extra point or argument to what you've already said. It functions very similarly to "moreover" or "plus." It is often placed at the beginning of a sentence or right after the verb.

English: on the contrary / quite the opposite
ExampleDanish: Jeg er ikke sur; tværtimod er jeg meget glad.
ExampleEnglish: I am not angry; on the contrary, I am very happy.
ContextualTip: Use this when you want to strongly contradict a previous statement or assumption. It’s often used to flip a negative statement into a positive one (or vice versa) for emphasis.

ExampleDanish: Det er usikkert, hvorvidt han kommer til festen.
ExampleEnglish: It is uncertain whether he is coming to the party.
ContextualTip: This word is used to introduce a choice between two possibilities (usually "whether or not"). It is more formal than om. While you can often use om instead, hvorvidt is perfect when you want to sound precise in writing or formal speech.

ExampleDanish: Han kan tale og spise samtidig.
ExampleEnglish: He can talk and eat at the same time.
ContextualTip: Links two things happening at once.This word can describe two things happening at once (simultaneously) or refer to something being "contemporary" (from the same time period). In spoken Danish, you will often hear it as samtidigt when used as an adverb.

ExampleDanish: Det bliver sandsynligvis regnvejr i morgen.
ExampleEnglish: It will probably rain tomorrow.
ContextualTip: This is a very common way to express probability. It is slightly more formal than nok, but less certain than helt sikkert. If you want to use it as an adjective (e.g., "a likely scenario"), you use the form sandsynlig.

ExampleDanish: Det bliver næppe regnvejr i dag.
ExampleEnglish: It will hardly rain today.
ContextualTip: Used when you don't think something will happen.This word is used to express strong doubt. It’s a slightly more formal way of saying "probably not" or "hardly." If you say "Jeg tror det næppe," you are saying "I highly doubt it."

ExampleDanish: Politiet valgte at anholde manden for tyveri.
ExampleEnglish: The police chose to arrest the man for theft.
ContextualTip: While at anholde is the standard term for a legal arrest by police, you might also hear at tilbageholde, which specifically means "to detain." Use anholde when someone is being officially charged and taken into custody.

English: to destroy / to ruin / to break
ExampleDanish: Stormen endte med at ødelægge hele haven.
ExampleEnglish: The storm ended up destroying the entire garden.
ContextualTip: This word is versatile! You can use it for physical destruction (like a building), but also for abstract things like a mood (at ødelægge stemningen) or a plan. If you just accidentally break a small object like a glass, Danes often use the verb at smadre or simply at gå i stykker (to go into pieces).

English: to create / to make / to cause
ExampleDanish: Kunstneren forsøger at skabe noget helt unikt.
ExampleEnglish: The artist is trying to create something completely unique.
ContextualTip: At skabe is generally used for creative or constructive acts. However, in slang, if you say someone is "at skabe sig," it means they are "making a scene" or behaving in an embarrassing, over-the-top way. Also, don't confuse it with the noun et skab, which means "a cupboard" or "a closet."

English: to damage / to harm / to hurt
ExampleDanish: Rygning kan at skade dit helbred.
ExampleEnglish: Smoking can damage your health.
ContextualTip: This is used for both physical injury to a person and material damage to an object. Note that the noun version is en skade (an injury/damage), which looks identical to the infinitive verb.

English: to provide / to obtain / to get
ExampleDanish: Kan du at skaffe to billetter til koncerten?
ExampleEnglish: Can you get two tickets for the concert?
ContextualTip: At skaffe implies a bit of effort or procurement. While at få simply means "to get/receive," at skaffe suggests you are actively sourcing something that might be difficult to find or requires an action to retrieve. In a workplace context, it is often used for "providing" results or materials.

ExampleDanish: Jeg er klar til at satse alt på denne idé.
ExampleEnglish: I am ready to bet everything on this idea.
ContextualTip: While it can mean literal gambling, it's very commonly used in business or personal life to mean "to prioritize" or "to go all in" on a specific strategy.

English: to move / to pull / to advance
ExampleDanish: Vi bliver nødt til at rykke hurtigt, hvis vi skal nå det.
ExampleEnglish: We have to move quickly if we are going to make it.
ContextualTip: This is a versatile word. It can mean physically moving an object, moving yourself (advancing), or even "pulling" someone to get their attention. In business, it's often used for "following up" on an invoice or a late task.

ExampleDanish: Det er svært at adskille de to ting fra hinanden.
ExampleEnglish: It is difficult to separate the two things from each other.
ContextualTip: Use this when physically separating objects or mentally distinguishing between concepts. If you want to say something "stands out," you can use the reflexive form: at adskille sig.

ExampleDanish: Jeg vil forsøge at gøre mit bedste.
ExampleEnglish: I will try to do my best.
ContextualTip: This is often used when you are making an effort or an attempt at something. It is slightly more formal than the word at prøve, which is also very common for "to try."

ExampleDanish: Jeg prøvede at advare ham om den glatte vej.
ExampleEnglish: I tried to warn him about the slippery road.
ContextualTip: This is used when giving a heads-up about a potential danger or negative consequence. You often "advare mod" (warn against) something or "advare om" (warn about) a situation.

English: to interrupt / to break / to disconnect
ExampleDanish: Undskyld, at jeg kommer til at afbryde dig.
ExampleEnglish: Sorry for interrupting you.
ContextualTip: This is used both for interrupting a conversation and for "breaking" or "disconnecting" something physical or technical, like a power supply or a phone call.

English: to give off / to deliver / to submit
ExampleDanish: Han skulle afgive sin forklaring til politiet.
ExampleEnglish: He had to give his statement to the police.
ContextualTip: This word is frequently used in formal or official situations. Common pairings include afgive en stemme (cast a vote), afgive et løfte (make a promise), or afgive varme (emit heat).

ExampleDanish: Der er ingen grund til bekymring.
ExampleEnglish: There is no reason for concern.
ContextualTip: This word covers both a general state of worry and specific "concerns." If you are talking about the plural (worries), it becomes bekymringer.

ExampleDanish: En stor andel af befolkningen bor i byerne.
ExampleEnglish: A large share of the population lives in the cities.
ContextualTip: This word is used for a part of a whole, often in terms of percentages or ownership. You'll also see it in the word andelsbolig, which is a specific type of cooperative housing common in Denmark.

ExampleDanish: Vi skal fordele opgaverne ligeligt imellem os.
ExampleEnglish: We need to distribute the tasks equally among us.
ContextualTip: This is used when spreading things out or sharing them among a group. Be careful: the plural noun fordele (advantages) looks exactly the same, but the verb is the act of handing things out or allocating resources.

Conjugation: fordelen (sing. def.) / fordele (pl. indef.) / fordelene (pl. def.)
ExampleDanish: Der er mange fordele ved at bo i byen.
ExampleEnglish: There are many advantages to living in the city.
ContextualTip: This is the plural form of en fordel. While the singular is fordel, the plural fordele is used frequently to discuss pros and cons (fordele og ulemper). It is spelled exactly like the verb "to distribute," so look for a noun marker like mange or en to tell them apart.

ExampleDanish: Der er både fordele og ulemper ved den nye plan
ExampleEnglish: There are both advantages and disadvantages to the new plan.
ContextualTip: This is the opposite of fordele. You will almost always hear them used together in the phrase "fordele og ulemper" (pros and cons).

English: to decide / to settle / to determine
ExampleDanish: Det er svært at afgøre, hvem der har ret.
ExampleEnglish: It is difficult to determine who is right.
ContextualTip: This word is stronger than "at beslutte" (to decide). It often implies reaching a final verdict, settling a dispute, or determining the outcome of a competition or case.

English: to hand in / to deliver / to return
ExampleDanish: Husk at aflevere bogen på biblioteket i morgen.
ExampleEnglish: Remember to return the book to the library tomorrow.
ContextualTip: This is used for handing something over to someone, whether it's an assignment to a teacher, a package to a customer, or returning a borrowed item. It implies a physical or formal transfer.

ExampleDanish: Vi skal have kaffen klar, og ydermere skal vi huske kagen.
ExampleEnglish: We need to have the coffee ready, and furthermore, we must remember the cake.
ContextualTip: This is a formal transition word. While it's perfect for essays or professional emails, you'll rarely hear it in casual conversation where Danes prefer to use "derudover" or just "og så."

ExampleDanish: Der er strenge betingelser for at få et lån i banken.
ExampleEnglish: There are strict conditions for getting a loan at the bank.
ContextualTip: en betingelse / betingelsen / betingelser / betingelserne

ExampleDanish: Det er en vigtig betingelse for aftalen.
ExampleEnglish: It is an important condition for the agreement.
ContextualTip: In Danish, betingelse is often used in the plural (betingelser) when referring to "terms and conditions" (vilkår og betingelser) in a contract or a set of rules.

ExampleDanish: Folk har mange forskellige holdninger til politik.
ExampleEnglish: People have many different opinions on politics.
ContextualTip: While holdning usually refers to a mental "attitude" or "opinion," it can also be used literally to describe someone's physical posture or carriage.

ExampleDanish: Det var en stor udfordring at lære dansk.
ExampleEnglish: it was a great challenge to learn danish.
ContextualTip: In a professional context, you'll often hear the phrase "at tage en udfordring op", which means "to take on a challenge" or "to accept a challenge."

English: besides / in addition to this / furthermore
ExampleDanish: Vi skal betale for lejen, og herudover kommer udgifter til el.
ExampleEnglish: We have to pay for the rent, and besides that, there are expenses for electricity.
ContextualTip: Herudover is a more formal way of saying "udover det" or "desuden." Use it when you want to sound professional or precise in your writing.

English: so / therefore / that is / i mean
ExampleDanish: Jeg er træt, altså går jeg i seng nu.
ExampleEnglish: i am tired, so i am going to bed now.
ContextualTip: Altså is one of the most versatile words in Danish. It can be used logically to mean "therefore," but in spoken Danish, it's often used as a filler word for emphasis (like "really!" or "honestly!") or to clarify a previous statement.

ExampleDanish: Jeg bekræfter hermed vores aftale.
ExampleEnglish: I hereby confirm our agreement.
ContextualTip: Hermed is a formal adverb frequently used in emails and official documents. It signals that the action is being performed by the very act of sending the message or document.

ExampleDanish: Han tog trøjen på omvendt.
ExampleEnglish: He put the sweater on inside out.
ContextualTip: Omvendt is used both for physical orientation (like wearing clothes backward or inside out) and for abstract concepts. When used as an adverb at the start of a sentence, it often translates to "conversely" or "on the other hand."

ExampleDanish: Hvad er den samlede omkostning for projektet?
ExampleEnglish: What is the total cost for the project?
ContextualTip: While pris (price) refers to the amount on a tag, omkostning is typically used for business expenses, production costs, or the broader "cost" of an action. In many contexts, it is used in the plural (omkostninger) to describe overhead or general expenses.

ExampleDanish: Den samlede pris er hundrede kroner.
ExampleEnglish: The total price is one hundred kroner.
ContextualTip: As an adjective, samlede is used when describing a whole amount in the definite form (preceded by den/det/de). It comes from the verb at samle, which you used previously in the past tense.

ExampleDanish: Vi har en moralsk forpligtelse til at hjælpe dem.
ExampleEnglish: We have a moral obligation to help them.
ContextualTip: This word comes from the verb at forpligte (to obligate/commit). While it often refers to legal or financial duties, it is very commonly used for social and moral "commitments" in Danish culture.

ExampleDanish: Jobbet kræver en høj grad af fleksibilitet.
ExampleEnglish: The job requires a high degree of flexibility.
ContextualTip: In a Danish workplace, fleksibilitet is a highly valued trait. It refers both to your ability to adapt to new tasks and the "flexibility" of your working hours (flextid).

ExampleDanish: Alle har rettighed til at ytre sig frit.
ExampleEnglish: Everyone has the right to express themselves freely.
ContextualTip: You will most often see this in the plural form, rettigheder (rights), especially when discussing menneskerettigheder (human rights) or borgerrettigheder (civil rights).

ExampleDanish: Det er nødvendigt at medbringe sit pas.
ExampleEnglish: It is necessary to bring one's passport.
ContextualTip: The word changes based on the noun it describes. You use nødvendigt with "et" words (like et skridt - a step) and nødvendig with "en" words (like en pause - a break).

ExampleDanish: Det er et acceptabelt resultat for alle parter.
ExampleEnglish: It is an acceptable result for all parties.
ContextualTip: Similar to "nødvendigt," this is the neuter (t-form) of the adjective. Note the spelling change in the plural/definite form (acceptable), where the "e" before the "l" drops out.

ExampleDanish: Jeg er bange for, at du misforstår mig.
ExampleEnglish: I am afraid that you misunderstand me.
ContextualTip: This is an irregular verb. Like the base verb at stå (to stand), the past tense changes the vowel significantly (stod). It is a common prefix-verb where "mis-" functions exactly like the English "mis-".

ExampleDanish: Det er vigtigt at overholde alle reglerne.
ExampleEnglish: It is important to comply with all the rules.
ContextualTip: This verb is frequently used in the context of deadlines (overholde en frist), promises (overholde et løfte), or laws. It literally translates to "over-hold," similar to "uphold" in English.

ExampleDanish: Han valgte at ignorere advarslen.
ExampleEnglish: He chose to ignore the warning.
ContextualTip: Like many Danish verbs ending in -ere, this word is derived from Latin and follows a very regular conjugation pattern. It is used both for intentionally snubbing a person and for disregarding information.

ExampleDanish: Jeg glemmer altid mine nøgler.
ExampleEnglish: I always forget my keys.
ContextualTip: This is a very common verb. If you want to say "don't forget," you say "glem ikke" or more commonly "husk" (remember). Unlike some other Danish verbs, "at glemme" is quite regular in its past tense pattern.

ExampleDanish: Som nævnt i går, er kontoret lukket på fredag.
ExampleEnglish: As mentioned yesterday, the office is closed on Friday.
ContextualTip: This is an extremely common transition phrase in both spoken and written Danish. It is the past participle of the verb at nævne (to mention). It is often used to refer back to a previous point in a conversation or document.

English: in return / on the other hand / conversely
ExampleDanish: Det er dyrt, men til gengæld er kvaliteten høj.
ExampleEnglish: It is expensive, but on the other hand, the quality is high.
ContextualTip: This phrase is a "balancer." It is used when you want to present a counterpoint that compensates for a previous statement. It can mean "in return" (quid pro quo) or "on the bright side" when following a negative remark.

ExampleDanish: Han vandt kampen og blev dermed verdensmester.
ExampleEnglish: He won the match and thereby became world champion.
ContextualTip: This is a formal transition word used to show a direct consequence of an action. It is a compound of der (there) and med (with). It is very common in professional and academic writing to link a cause to an effect.

ExampleDanish: Beslutningen fik en alvorlig konsekvens.
ExampleEnglish: The decision had a serious consequence.
ContextualTip: Note that "konsekvens" in Danish can refer both to a result/effect and to the trait of being "consistent" or "firm" (at være konsekvent). When you wrote "konsekven," it was likely a typo for the singular noun konsekvens.

ExampleDanish: Der er ingen grund til at være bekymret.
ExampleEnglish: There is no reason to be worried.
ContextualTip: This word is versatile. It can mean the "reason" for something, the physical "ground" beneath you, or a "plot of land" where you build a house. It is also the root of the word grundlæggende (fundamental).

ExampleDanish: Det er et stærkt argument for planen.
ExampleEnglish: It is a strong argument for the plan.
ContextualTip: In Danish, et argument refers specifically to a piece of reasoning or a point in a debate. If you mean "argument" in the sense of a verbal fight or disagreement, the word you want is et skænderi.

ExampleDanish: Han ville ikke indrømme, at han tog fejl.
ExampleEnglish: He would not admit that he was wrong.
ContextualTip: This verb is used both for admitting a mistake and for "conceding" a point in a debate. It can also be used as indrømmet (admittedly) at the beginning of a sentence to acknowledge a fact before making a counterpoint.

ExampleDanish: De var nødt til at afvise hans anmodning.
ExampleEnglish: They had to reject his request.
ContextualTip: This verb is very versatile. You can afvise an idea, a person at the door, or a claim. It carries a sense of "dismissing" something or someone. Note the past tense afviste, which follows a common pattern for verbs with a long vowel ending in "s".

English: to claim / to assert / to insist
ExampleDanish: Han valgte at påstå, at han aldrig havde set brevet.
ExampleEnglish: He chose to claim that he had never seen the letter.
ContextualTip: This is an irregular verb. Like the verb at stå (to stand), the past tense changes the vowel to stod. It is often used when someone makes a statement that hasn't been proven yet or when someone "insists" on a specific version of the truth.

ExampleDanish: Det er vigtigt at fastholde fokus på opgaven.
ExampleEnglish: It is important to maintain focus on the task.
ContextualTip: This is an irregular verb derived from at holde (to hold). It is used both physically (to hold someone/something firmly) and figuratively (to maintain a point of view, a position, or a grip on a situation).

ExampleDanish: Han gjorde det således, som han var blevet instrueret.
ExampleEnglish: He did it thus, as he had been instructed.
ContextualTip: This is a more formal version of sådan. You will often see it in written instructions or formal speech to describe how something is done or to sum up a conclusion (meaning "therefore").

ExampleDanish: Jeg kan godt lide frugt, især æbler.
ExampleEnglish: I like fruit, especially apples.
ContextualTip: This word is a contraction of i sær (in separate). It is used to single out one thing from a group. You can also use særligt as a synonym in many contexts.

ExampleDanish: Det regner, og derfor bliver vi inde.
ExampleEnglish: It is raining, and therefore we are staying inside.
ContextualTip: This is one of the most common causal adverbs in Danish. It links a cause to an effect. Unlike the English "therefore," which can feel a bit formal, derfor is used constantly in everyday spoken Danish to explain the reason for an action.

ExampleDanish: Han hilste på gæsterne, og de hilste ligeledes på ham.
ExampleEnglish: He greeted the guests, and they likewise greeted him.
ContextualTip: This is a formal alternative to også (also) or på samme måde (in the same way). It is very common in written Danish or professional correspondence to avoid repeating the word "også."

ExampleDanish: Jeg glemte min pung, men heldigvis havde jeg min telefon.
ExampleEnglish: I forgot my wallet, but luckily I had my phone.
ContextualTip: This is an adverb used to express relief about a situation. It is a compound of heldig (lucky) and vis (manner/way). In a sentence, it often moves to the beginning or follows the verb depending on the emphasis.

ExampleDanish: Der er en stor gevinst ved at motionere hver dag.
ExampleEnglish: There is a great benefit to exercising every day.
ContextualTip: While gevinst is often used for a "prize" in a lottery or competition, it is also frequently used in business and health contexts to describe a "gain" or "benefit." If you are talking specifically about financial profit, you might also hear the word overskud.

ExampleDanish: Den største forhindring for projektet er mangel på tid.
ExampleEnglish: The biggest obstacle for the project is a lack of time.
ContextualTip: This word comes from the verb at forhindre (to prevent). It can refer to both physical obstacles, like those in a forhindringsbane (obstacle course), and abstract or metaphorical barriers in life or work.

ExampleDanish: Politiet kender endnu ikke den præcise årsag til ulykken.
ExampleEnglish: The police do not yet know the exact cause of the accident.
ContextualTip: While årsag and grund both mean "reason," årsag is more specifically used for "cause" (cause and effect). You also see it in the common phrase "Ingen årsag", which means "No problem" or "You're welcome" (literally: "No cause [for thanks]").

English: to recognize / to acknowledge / to admit
ExampleDanish: Han måtte til sidst erkende sine fejl.
ExampleEnglish: He eventually had to acknowledge his mistakes.
ContextualTip: While at indrømme is often used for admitting a specific action (like a crime or a lie), at erkende is more about a deeper realization or an intellectual acknowledgment of a truth or a situation. It is also the word used for "recognizing" a legal responsibility.

English: to assess / to evaluate / to estimate
ExampleDanish: Eksperten skal vurderer skadens omfang.
ExampleEnglish: The expert must assess the extent of the damage.
ContextualTip: This verb is a "thinker's" word. It is used when you are making a judgment based on evidence or observation. It is the root of the noun en vurdering (an assessment/appraisal).

ExampleDanish: Det er urimeligt, at vi skal arbejde i weekenden.
ExampleEnglish: It is unreasonable that we have to work during the weekend.
ContextualTip: This word is the opposite of rimeligt (reasonable). The suffix -t in your prompt indicates it is either describing a neuter noun (et urimeligt krav) or acting as an adverb to describe an action. It is a very common word to use when expressing frustration about a situation you find unjust.

ExampleDanish: Det er vanskeligt at lære et nyt sprog på kort tid.
ExampleEnglish: It is difficult to learn a new language in a short time.
ContextualTip: While svært is the most common everyday word for "difficult," vanskeligt is slightly more formal and often refers to something that is complicated or requires a high degree of effort to solve. The -t ending here suggests it is describing a situation or a neuter noun.

ExampleDanish: Vi kommer muligvis lidt senere i aften.
ExampleEnglish: We will possibly arrive a little later tonight.
ContextualTip: This is a very common adverb used to express uncertainty. While måske (maybe) is often used at the beginning of a sentence, muligvis is frequently placed after the verb. It is slightly more formal than måske but still used regularly in speech.

ExampleDanish: Jeg har en plan for, hvordan vi løser problemet.
ExampleEnglish: I have a plan for how we solve the problem.
ContextualTip: This refers to a strategy or a scheme. Note that "plan" can also mean "level" or "floor" in a technical or geometric context (e.g., et arkitektonisk plan).

ExampleDanish: Jeg vil varmt anbefale denne film.
ExampleEnglish: I would highly recommend this movie.
ContextualTip: In Denmark, you’ll often hear this word in restaurants or shops when asking for a "recommendation" (en anbefaling). It is a very common way to politely share a positive opinion.

ExampleDanish: Hun anbragte vasen på bordet.
ExampleEnglish: She placed the vase on the table.
ContextualTip: Anbringe is a bit more formal than the common word stille or sætte. It is often used when you are placing something carefully in a specific spot or when talking about "investing" or "depositing" money (anbringe penge).

English: suspect / sense / have a hint of
ExampleDanish: Jeg aner ikke, hvor mine nøgler er.
ExampleEnglish: I have no idea where my keys are.
ContextualTip: The phrase "Jeg aner det ikke" is a very common, idiomatic way to say "I haven't the slightest clue." It is slightly more emphatic than just saying "Jeg ved det ikke" (I don't know).

English: to recognize / to acknowledge / to appreciate
ExampleDanish: De anerkendte hans store indsats for firmaet.
ExampleEnglish: They recognized his great effort for the company.
ContextualTip: Anerkende is used both for "recognizing" a fact or a country's sovereignty and for "giving credit" or showing appreciation for someone's work. It implies a level of formal validation or respect.

ExampleDanish: Han valgte at anmelde tyveriet til politiet.
ExampleEnglish: He chose to report the theft to the police.
ContextualTip: Anmelde is a versatile word. It is used when you "report" a crime to the authorities, but also when a critic "reviews" a book, movie, or restaurant. A reviewer is called en anmelder.

English: to consider / to regard / to view
ExampleDanish: Jeg anser det for at være en stor ære.
ExampleEnglish: I consider it to be a great honor.
ContextualTip: Anse is often used with the preposition for. If you want to say you "consider someone to be" something, you would say "Jeg anser ham for (at være)..." It is a slightly more formal way of expressing an opinion or evaluation compared to synes.

ExampleDanish: Firmaet har planer om at ansætte fem nye medarbejdere.
ExampleEnglish: The company plans to hire five new employees.
ContextualTip: Ansætte is specifically used for hiring people. If you are talking about "hiring" or "renting" an object (like a car or a tuxedo), you should use the word leje instead.

English: to use / to apply / to utilize
ExampleDanish: Man kan anvende denne metode til mange formål.
ExampleEnglish: One can use this method for many purposes.
ContextualTip: Anvende is more formal than the common word bruge. You will often see it in technical manuals, scientific contexts, or formal instructions where a specific "application" or "method" is being discussed.

ExampleDanish: Hvad er formålet med dette møde?
ExampleEnglish: What is the purpose of this meeting?
ContextualTip: Formål is a very useful word in professional or academic settings. If you want to say something is "meaningless," you can say it is formålsløst. Whereas mål usually refers to a specific "goal" or "target" (like a finish line or a soccer goal), formål refers to the underlying reason or intent.

ExampleDanish: Først spiser vi middag, og dernæst går vi i biografen.
ExampleEnglish: First we eat dinner, and afterwards we go to the cinema.
ContextualTip: Dernæst is a transition word used to list things in a sequence. It is slightly more formal than bagefter or så. It is perfect for professional presentations or recipes when you are moving from one step to the next.

ExampleDanish: Hendes største ønske er at rejse til Japan.
ExampleEnglish: Her greatest wish is to travel to Japan.
ContextualTip: When talking about a "wish list" (for a birthday or Christmas), Danes call it en ønskeseddel. While ønske is the noun, remember that the verb form often uses sig when you want a specific gift for yourself.

ExampleDanish: Det var et rigtig godt råd, du gav mig.
ExampleEnglish: It was a really good piece of advice you gave me.
ContextualTip: In Danish, et råd is countable, so you can say "one advice" or "two advices" (to råd), unlike in English where "advice" is uncountable. As we touched on earlier, this same word is used for administrative bodies like Sikkerhedsrådet (The Security Council).

ExampleDanish: Besøget havde en varighed af to timer.
ExampleEnglish: The visit had a duration of two hours.
ContextualTip: Varighed comes from the verb at vare (to last). It is commonly used in formal contexts, such as describing the length of a contract, a movie, or a medical treatment. If something is permanent, you might describe it as being of "evig varighed" (eternal duration).

ExampleDanish: Det afhænger af vejret.
ExampleEnglish: It depends on the weather.
ContextualTip: Afhænge is almost always followed by the preposition af (of). If you want to describe someone who is "addicted" or "dependent," you use the related adjective afhængig. While the conjugation "afhængede" exists, you will mostly encounter this verb in the present tense (afhænger).

ExampleDanish: Børnene i klassen udviste en stor spørgelyst.
ExampleEnglish: The children in the class showed a great eagerness to ask questions.
ContextualTip: Spørgelyst is a compound word combining spørge (to ask) and lyst (desire/lust). It is a very positive word used to describe someone who is inquisitive or curious in an active, engaged way. You'll often hear it in educational settings or at the end of a presentation.

English: to distribute / to hand out / to dispense
ExampleDanish: Læreren valgte at uddele opgaverne i begyndelsen af timen.
ExampleEnglish: The teacher chose to hand out the assignments at the beginning of the lesson.
ContextualTip: Uddele is a compound of ud (out) and dele (share/divide). It is the standard word for distributing physical items, like flyers or awards, but it can also be used for "handing out" punishments or responsibilities in a more formal tone.

ExampleDanish: Skeen er lavet af ægte sølv.
ExampleEnglish: The spoon is made of real silver.
ContextualTip: It looks like you might have had a small typo (søvl instead of sølv)! In Denmark, sølv is not just the metal; it's also the standard word for "silverware" or "cutlery" (sølvtøj). If you are looking for the word for a "soul," that would be en sjæl.

ExampleDanish: Vi må tage hensyn til naboerne.
ExampleEnglish: We must show consideration for the neighbors.
ContextualTip: The most common way to use this word is in the phrase at tage hensyn til, which means "to take into account" or "to be considerate of." If you describe someone as hensynsfuld, you are saying they are a very thoughtful or considerate person.

ExampleDanish: Flere og flere familier vælger at tage mere hensyn til miljøet end de har gjort før.
ExampleEnglish: More and more families are choosing to be more environmentally conscious than they have been in the past.
ContextualTip: Miljø is used both for the natural environment (nature/climate) and for a social environment (the atmosphere at work or in a specific group). If someone is environmentally friendly, they are described as miljøvenlig.,,,

English: to meet / to hit / to make (a decision)
ExampleDanish: Vi skal træffe en vigtig beslutning i dag.
ExampleEnglish: We have to make an important decision today.
ContextualTip: Træffe is a high-frequency verb with two main uses. It can mean "to meet" someone (often by chance or appointment), but its most common formal use is in the phrase at træffe en beslutning (to make/reach a decision). Note the irregular past tense traf.

English: to lay / to put / to place
ExampleDanish: Vil du være sød at lægge bogen på bordet?
ExampleEnglish: Would you be so kind as to put the book on the table?
ContextualTip: Lægge is often confused with ligge. A good rule of thumb is that lægge involves movement (placing something down horizontally), while ligge describes a stationary position (lying there). Use lægge when you are the one doing the action to an object.

ExampleDanish: Det er dit ansvar at lukke vinduerne.
ExampleEnglish: It is your responsibility to close the windows.
ContextualTip: Ansvar is often used with the verb at tage (to take responsibility) or at have (to have responsibility). If you want to describe someone as "responsible," you add the suffix -bevidst to get ansvarsbevidst, which is a very common positive trait to list on a Danish CV.

ExampleDanish: Hans udsagn i retten blev betvivlet af vidnerne.
ExampleEnglish: His statement in court was questioned by the witnesses.
ContextualTip: Udsagn is a formal word often used in legal, journalistic, or logical contexts. It literally translates to something "said out" (ud-sagn). In grammar, you might also encounter the word udsagnsord, which is the Danish term for a "verb."

ExampleDanish: Der er ikke overensstemmelse mellem det, han siger, og det, han gør.
ExampleEnglish: There is no consistency between what he says and what he does.
ContextualTip: This word is most commonly used in the phrase i overensstemmelse med (in accordance with). It is a formal term often found in legal documents, contracts, or when discussing whether two pieces of information match up. It literally stems from "agreeing voices" or "tuning together."

ExampleDanish: "At kaste håndklædet i ringen" er et kendt dansk udtryk.
ExampleEnglish: "To throw the towel in the ring" is a well-known Danish expression.
ContextualTip: Udtryk can refer to both a linguistic phrase and a facial expression. If you want to say someone looks a certain way, you can talk about their ansigtsudtryk (facial expression). Like many other neuter nouns (et) ending in a consonant, the indefinite plural is the same as the singular.

ExampleDanish: Vores primære mål er at vinde kampen.
ExampleEnglish: Our primary goal is to win the match.
ContextualTip: In Danish, primært is often used as an adverb (meaning "primarily"), while primær or primære are used as adjectives. Use it when discussing the most important or fundamental aspect of something.

ExampleDanish: Det er et sjældent syn i Danmark.
ExampleEnglish: It is a rare sight in Denmark.
ContextualTip: Sjældent is used both as an adjective (a rare thing) and an adverb (how often something happens). If you are describing a plural noun, remember to use the form sjældne (e.g., "sjældne mønter" - rare coins).

ExampleDanish: Jeg mødte ham tilfældigvis i supermarkedet.
ExampleEnglish: I met him by chance in the supermarket.
ContextualTip: Tilfældigvis is often used to ask polite questions, similar to the English "Do you happen to...?" (e.g., "Ved du tilfældigvis, hvad klokken er?" - Do you happen to know what time it is?).

ExampleDanish: Hendes mening har stor indflydelse på projektet.
ExampleEnglish: Her opinion has a great influence on the project.
ContextualTip: Indflydelse is often used with the preposition på (on). If you want to say someone is "under the influence" (usually regarding alcohol), the Danish phrase is "under indflydelse af."

English: to involve / to lead to / to result in
ExampleDanish: Den nye lov vil medføre store ændringer for borgerne.
ExampleEnglish: The new law will lead to major changes for the citizens.
ContextualTip: Medføre is a formal word often used in professional or academic contexts to describe a logical consequence or a result of an action. It is essentially the Danish equivalent of "to bring about."

ExampleDanish: Vi kan ikke udelukke muligheden for regn.
ExampleEnglish: We cannot rule out the possibility of rain.
ContextualTip: Udelukke is frequently used in professional or scientific contexts when "ruling out" a hypothesis or a candidate. It can also mean "to shut someone out" in a social sense.

ExampleDanish: Kan du forklare mig, hvordan det virker?
ExampleEnglish: Can you explain to me how it works?
ContextualTip: Forklare is often used with the preposition for when explaining something to someone (e.g., "Jeg forklarede det for ham"). If you are making an excuse, you might use the noun form en forklaring (an explanation).

ExampleDanish: Jeg skylder dig en stor tjeneste.
ExampleEnglish: I owe you a big favor.
ContextualTip: Be careful not to confuse this with at skyldes (to be caused by). At skylde is used when you owe money or gratitude to someone. If you owe money, you are i gæld.

English: be due to / be caused by
ExampleDanish: Forsinkelsen skyldes de dårlige vejrforhold.
ExampleEnglish: The delay is due to the bad weather conditions.
ContextualTip: Skyldes is a passive-only verb (a deponent verb). It is used to point to a cause or reason. Don't confuse it with the noun skyld (guilt/fault) or the verb at skylde (to owe money or a favor).

ExampleDanish: Jeg laver mad, imens du dækker bordet.
ExampleEnglish: I am cooking while you set the table.
ContextualTip: Imens is primarily used to describe two actions happening at the same time. While it is often interchangeable with mens, "imens" is frequently used when it stands alone as an adverb meaning "meanwhile" (e.g., "Gå du bare, jeg bliver her imens").

English: to require / to demand / to claim
ExampleDanish: Arbejdet kræver stor koncentration.
ExampleEnglish: The work requires great concentration.
ContextualTip: Kræve is used both for objective requirements (like a skill needed for a job) and for personal demands (like a person demanding an explanation). It can also be used in the context of "claiming" lives, such as in a natural disaster.

ExampleDanish: Han forsøgte at bevise sin uskyld i retten.
ExampleEnglish: He tried to prove his innocence in court.
ContextualTip: Bevise is used when you provide evidence for a claim or a theory. The noun form is et bevis (a piece of evidence/proof). In mathematics, you also use bevise to describe the process of completing a formal proof.

ExampleDanish: Det er et meget nyttigt værktøj til arbejdet.
ExampleEnglish: It is a very useful tool for work.
ContextualTip: "Nyttig" is often used for things that serve a practical purpose. If you are describing a person who is helpful, you might prefer "hjælpsom," though "nyttig" can be used for a "useful" contributor in a more functional sense.

ExampleDanish: Det var netop det, jeg mente.
ExampleEnglish: That was exactly what I meant.
ContextualTip: "Netop" is incredibly versatile. It can mean "exactly" (as in "precisely so") or "just" in a temporal sense (e.g., "jeg er netop kommet" - "I have just arrived"). It's a very common filler word to show agreement in conversation.

English: to take a gap year / to take a break from studies or work
ExampleDanish: Mange vælger at sabbatte efter gymnasiet.
ExampleEnglish: Many choose to take a gap year after high school.
ContextualTip: This verb is derived from "sabbatår" (gap year). It is a colloquial way to describe the act of taking a long break, usually between finishing one level of education and starting the next.

ExampleDanish: Han rejser rundt i verden i sit sabbatår.
ExampleEnglish: He is traveling around the world during his gap year.
ContextualTip: In Denmark, it is very common for students to take one or two "sabbatår" between gymnasium (high school) and university to work or travel.

ExampleDanish: Eleverne var meget opmærksomme under timen.
ExampleEnglish: The students were very attentive during the lesson.
ContextualTip: This word is often used with the preposition "på" (opmærksom på) to mean "aware of" or "paying attention to." For example: "Jeg er opmærksom på problemet."

English: regardless of / no matter / irrespective of
ExampleDanish: Vi tager på stranden uanset vejret.
ExampleEnglish: We are going to the beach regardless of the weather.
ContextualTip: It is often used with "om" (uanset om...) to mean "regardless of whether..." or "no matter if...". It stays the same regardless of the sentence structure.

ExampleDanish: Du bør læse op til din prøve.
ExampleEnglish: You ought to study for your test.
ContextualTip: As a modal verb, "bør" is followed directly by the infinitive without "at." It is most often used to express a moral obligation, a recommendation, or something that is advisable.

ExampleDanish: Huset er billigt, og derudover er det i god stand.
ExampleEnglish: The house is cheap, and furthermore it is in good condition.
ContextualTip: It is a common conjunctive adverb used to add information or arguments. It literally translates to "there-out-over," helping you transition between two related points.

ExampleDanish: Det var koldt, men vi gik dog en tur.
ExampleEnglish: It was cold, but we went for a walk anyway.
ContextualTip: In Danish, "dog" is most commonly used as a transition word similar to "however." Be careful not to confuse it with the English word for animal, which in Danish is "en hund.

ExampleDanish: Butikken sælger frugt, såsom æbler og pærer.
ExampleEnglish: The shop sells fruit, such as apples and pears.
ContextualTip: "Såsom" is primarily used to introduce specific examples of a broader category. It is slightly more formal than just using "som" or "for eksempel."

English: thereby / by that / by doing so
ExampleDanish: Han trykkede på knappen og derved startede maskinen.
ExampleEnglish: He pressed the button and thereby started the machine.
ContextualTip: "Derved" is a formal adverb used to indicate the means or cause by which something happens. It is a compound of "der" (there) and "ved" (by/at), literally meaning "by that." It is very common in technical or formal descriptions to explain a logical consequence.

ExampleDanish: Det skyldes dels manglende tid, dels manglende penge.
ExampleEnglish: It is due partly to a lack of time, and partly to a lack of money.
ContextualTip: "Dels" is almost always used in pairs (dels... dels...) to list two or more reasons, factors, or parts of a whole. It functions similarly to the English "partly... partly..." or "on the one hand... on the other hand..." within a single sentence.

ExampleDanish: Vi skal have både kage og kaffe til mødet.
ExampleEnglish: We are going to have both cake and coffee for the meeting.
ContextualTip: "Både" is almost always followed by "og" (både... og...) to link two items. Note that if you are referring to a boat, it is a noun ("en båd"), but this conjunction is very common for connecting ideas or things.

ExampleDanish: Jeg har mødt ham tidligere på dagen.
ExampleEnglish: I have met him earlier today.
ContextualTip: "Tidligere" is the comparative form of "tidlig" (early), but it is very frequently used as an adverb to mean "previously" or "in the past." It doesn't change regardless of the gender or number of the noun it describes.

ExampleDanish: Vi har et fælles ansvar for at holde haven ren.
ExampleEnglish: We have a shared responsibility to keep the garden clean.
ContextualTip: This word is used for things shared by a group. It is often found in compound words like "fællesskab" (community) or "fællesnævner" (common denominator).

ExampleDanish: Der er ingen forskel på kønnene i denne undersøgelse.
ExampleEnglish: There is no difference between the genders in this study.
ContextualTip: In grammar, "køn" refers to whether a noun is "fælleskøn" (en-words) or "intetkøn" (et-words). It is also the root of the adjective "køn," which means "pretty" or "handsome."

ExampleDanish: Det kan være svært at ændre sine kostvaner i en travl hverdag.
ExampleEnglish: It can be difficult to change one's dietary habits in a busy everyday life.
ContextualTip: This is a compound noun made of "kost" (diet/food) and "vane" (habit). In Denmark, it is frequently used in health and nutrition contexts, often appearing in the plural form "kostvaner."

ExampleDanish: Mange studerende arbejder på deltid ved siden af deres studier.
ExampleEnglish: Many students work part-time alongside their studies.
ContextualTip: In Denmark, "deltid" is commonly used in job advertisements. If you are looking for full-time work, the opposite term is heltid. It is often used as a prefix in words like deltidsansat (part-time employee) or deltidsjob.

English: to confirm / to verify / to acknowledge
ExampleDanish: Systemet sender en e-mail for at bekræfte din reservation.
ExampleEnglish: The system sends an email to confirm your reservation.
ContextualTip: In technical contexts, like software development, "bekræft" is the standard word for "confirm" buttons. It is also commonly used in formal communication to acknowledge that a message or document has been received.

ExampleDanish: Danmark er kendt for at have en høj grad af ligestilling på arbejdsmarkedet.
ExampleEnglish: Denmark is known for having a high degree of equality in the labor market.
ContextualTip: While it can mean equality in general, "ligestilling" is most commonly used in the context of "køn" (gender). For other types of equality, such as social or legal equality, the word lighed is often used instead.

ExampleDanish: Du kan følge med i projektets udvikling på vores hjemmeside.
ExampleEnglish: You can follow the project's development on our website.
ContextualTip: "At følge" is often used in the phrase "at følge med," which means "to keep up" or "to stay updated." In a technical context, it is also the word used for "following" someone on social media.

ExampleDanish: Der er kommet en større bevidsthed om miljøet i de seneste år.
ExampleEnglish: There has been a greater awareness of the environment in recent years.
ContextualTip: This noun is derived from the adjective bevidst (conscious/aware). It is often used in terms like miljøbevidsthed (environmental awareness) or samfundsbevidsthed (social consciousness).

ExampleDanish: Der er altid meget trængsel i den offentlige transport i myldretiden.
ExampleEnglish: There is always a lot of congestion in public transport during rush hour.
ContextualTip: This word is commonly used to describe "traffic congestion" (trafikal trængsel) or crowded places. It can also be used figuratively in a religious or formal context to mean "hardship" or "tribulation," though in daily life, it usually refers to crowds.

ExampleDanish: Der er ingen grænse for, hvad vi kan opnå sammen.
ExampleEnglish: There is no limit to what we can achieve together.
ContextualTip: In Denmark, "grænsen" often refers specifically to the border with Germany. It is also used abstractly to describe personal boundaries or technical limits in software and physics.

ExampleDanish: Det var et uheld, at glasset gik i stykker.
ExampleEnglish: It was an accident that the glass broke.
ContextualTip: "Uheld" is often used for minor accidents or bad luck. For serious accidents, especially those involving traffic or injuries, the word ulykke is more commonly used.

ExampleDanish: Vi skal arbejde videre med projektet i morgen.
ExampleEnglish: We will continue working on the project tomorrow.
ContextualTip: This word is incredibly versatile in Danish. It is often used with verbs to mean "continue" (e.g., gå videre - go on/proceed). In a technical or navigation context, it is the standard word for "next" or "continue" buttons.

ExampleDanish: Det er vigtigt at have fokus på medarbejdernes trivsel.
ExampleEnglish: It is important to focus on the employees' well-being.
ContextualTip: "Trivsel" is a key term in Danish culture, especially in work and school environments. It refers to a holistic state of feeling good, being productive, and thriving socially. It is often used in the context of a trivselsundersøgelse (well-being survey).

ExampleDanish: Arbejdsopgaverne er jævnt fordelt mellem alle i teamet.
ExampleEnglish: The work tasks are evenly distributed among everyone in the team.
ContextualTip: This is the past participle of the verb at fordele (to distribute). It is very common in professional and technical contexts when discussing how resources, weight, or tasks are allocated. For example, "fordelt over tid" means "spread over time."

ExampleDanish: Lægen gav ham en alvorlig besked om hans helbred.
ExampleEnglish: The doctor gave him a serious message about his health.
ContextualTip: This adjective is used for both people's temperaments (a serious person) and the severity of a situation (a serious accident). In a professional context, it often describes the weight or importance of a task or decision.

ExampleDanish: Det er vigtigt at vælge en hensigtsmæssig løsning på problemet.
ExampleEnglish: It is important to choose an appropriate solution to the problem.
ContextualTip: This word describes something that is "fit for purpose." It comes from hensigt (intention/purpose) and mæssig (in accordance with). In a professional or technical setting, it is the go-to word for saying something is logical or effective for the current goal.

ExampleDanish: Der er stadig flest mandlige ansøgere til stillingen som softwareudvikler.
ExampleEnglish: There are still mostly male applicants for the position as a software developer.
ContextualTip: This adjective refers specifically to biological sex or gender. For things traditionally associated with "manliness" or "masculinity" in a stylistic sense, the word maskulin is often used instead. If you are talking about "a man" as a noun, it is en mand.

ExampleDanish: Mange programmører foretrækker at arbejde i et roligt miljø.
ExampleEnglish: Many programmers prefer to work in a quiet environment.
ContextualTip: This verb is often used with the preposition frem for when comparing two things, for example: "Jeg foretrækker kaffe frem for te" (I prefer coffee over tea). Note the irregular past tense form foretrak.

ExampleDanish: Regeringen vil indføre nye regler for affaldssortering til næste år.
ExampleEnglish: The government wants to introduce new rules for waste sorting next year.
ContextualTip: This is a very versatile verb. It is used for bringing in new laws (indføre love), implementing new systems in software, or importing goods from abroad. It literally means "to lead in."

English: to raise / to increase / to lift
ExampleDanish: Butikken har valgt at hæve priserne på grund af inflationen.
ExampleEnglish: The store has chosen to raise the prices because of inflation.
ContextualTip: In Denmark, you will most often hear this word in three contexts: banking (withdrawing cash), baking (letting dough rise), or when discussing an injury where something is "hævet" (swollen). It is also used formally when a meeting is adjourned: "Mødet er hævet."

ExampleDanish: Det er vigtigt at bruge ægte materialer, når man bygger noget holdbart.
ExampleEnglish: It is important to use genuine materials when building something durable.
ContextualTip: This word is unique because it usually doesn't change form regardless of the gender or number of the noun it describes (it is indeclinable). In a professional context, you might use it to describe "authentic" data or "genuine" feedback. It is also used to describe someone who is sincere or "the real deal."

ExampleDanish: Jeg vågner altid meget tidligt om morgenen.
ExampleEnglish: I always wake up very early in the morning.
ContextualTip: "Tidlig" is often used with "om" when referring to times of the day (e.g., "tidligt om morgenen" or "tidligt om aftenen"). When used as an adverb to describe an action, remember to add the -t to make it "tidligt".

ExampleDanish: Er du allerede færdig med dit arbejde?
ExampleEnglish: Are you already finished with your work?
ContextualTip: "Allerede" is often used to express surprise that something happened sooner than expected. In sentences, it usually follows the verb unless the verb is at the end of the clause.

ExampleDanish: Som regel drikker jeg kaffe til morgenmad.
ExampleEnglish: As a rule, I drink coffee for breakfast.
ContextualTip: This phrase is a very common way to describe habits or things that happen most of the time. In a sentence, "som regel" usually occupies the adverbial slot; if you start a sentence with it, remember the inversion rule (the verb must come next).

English: occasionally / at times / from time to time
ExampleDanish: Vi går lejlighedsvis ud og spiser på restaurant.
ExampleEnglish: Occasionally, we go out to eat at a restaurant.
ContextualTip: This word is slightly more formal than "nogle gange" (sometimes). It is formed from "lejlighed" (occasion/opportunity) and the suffix "-vis" (which corresponds to "-wise" or "-ly" in English to form adverbs). In a sentence, it often follows the verb due to Danish word order rules.

ExampleDanish: Jeg kommer straks!
ExampleEnglish: I am coming right away!
ContextualTip: "Straks" implies a sense of urgency and is often used when an action happens without any delay. While "nu" means "now," "straks" emphasizes that something will happen the very next second. If you use it to start a sentence, remember the inversion: "Straks kom han ind i stuen."

ExampleDanish: Butikkerne i denne by lukker almindeligvis klokken atten.
ExampleEnglish: The shops in this city usually close at eighteen o'clock.
ContextualTip: Almindeligvis is a synonym for som regel. It is derived from the adjective almindelig (common/ordinary). Use it when you want to describe what happens under normal or general circumstances. If you start a sentence with this word, remember to swap the subject and verb: "Almindeligvis spiser jeg..."

ExampleDanish: Det regner hyppigt i løbet af efteråret.
ExampleEnglish: It rains frequently during the autumn.
ContextualTip: Hyppigt is the adverbial form of the adjective hyppig. While it is a synonym for ofte, it often sounds a bit more formal or precise. It is commonly used in technical, medical, or statistical contexts to describe the frequency of an event.

ExampleDanish: Ingenting varer evigt.
ExampleEnglish: Nothing lasts forever.
ContextualTip: Evigt is the adverbial form of the adjective evig (eternal). It is very common in the phrase "for evigt" (forever). In daily speech, Danes often use it as an intensifier to express annoyance, similar to "constantly," for example: "Han klager evigt og altid" (He is always and forever complaining).

ExampleDanish: Min bror kan lide te, hvorimod jeg foretrækker kaffe.
ExampleEnglish: My brother likes tea, whereas I prefer coffee.
ContextualTip: Hvorimod is a coordinating conjunction used to contrast two different facts or situations. It is slightly more formal than mens (while) when used for comparison. Note that it typically introduces a subordinate clause, but in Danish, it does not require a change in word order in the clause that follows it.

ExampleDanish: Ring til mig, ifald du bliver forsinket.
ExampleEnglish: Call me in case you are delayed.
ContextualTip: Ifald is a slightly formal or old-fashioned synonym for hvis or i tilfælde af. You won't hear it as often in casual speech, but it is frequently seen in formal writing, legal texts, or older literature. It functions as a conjunction that introduces a condition.

English: to limit / to restrict / to reduce
ExampleDanish: Vi skal begrænse vores brug af plastik.
ExampleEnglish: We must limit our use of plastic.
ContextualTip: "Begrænse" is often used when talking about setting boundaries, cutting down on resources, or narrowing down options (e.g., "begrænse udvalget" - limit the selection).

English: to prevent / to hinder / to stop
ExampleDanish: Regnen kunne ikke forhindre os i at tage i skoven.
ExampleEnglish: The rain could not prevent us from going to the forest.
ContextualTip: "Forhindre" is typically used when stopping an event, action, or disaster from happening. It is often paired with the preposition "i" (prevent someone from doing something).

ExampleDanish: Hun sendte sin ansøgning til firmaet i går.
ExampleEnglish: She sent her application to the company yesterday.
ContextualTip: "Ansøgning" is usually used in formal contexts, like applying for a job (jobansøgning) or admission to a university (optagelsesansøgning).

ExampleDanish: Vi skal bruge det rigtige udstyr til turen.
ExampleEnglish: We need to use the right equipment for the trip.
ContextualTip: "Udstyr" is a collective noun and is typically used in the singular form to describe all the tools or gear needed for a specific activity (e.g., sportsudstyr or it-udstyr).

English: to adapt / to adjust / to customize
ExampleDanish: Du skal tilpasse din hastighed efter vejret.
ExampleEnglish: You must adjust your speed according to the weather.
ContextualTip: "Tilpasse" is often used with the reflexive pronoun "sig" (tilpasse sig) to mean "to adapt oneself" to a new situation, culture, or environment.

ExampleDanish: Rejsen vil berige dit liv med nye oplevelser.
ExampleEnglish: The journey will enrich your life with new experiences.
ContextualTip: "Berige" can be used both literally (e.g., enriching uranium) and abstractly/metaphorically (e.g., enriching a conversation or someone's knowledge).

ExampleDanish: De nye regler vil besværliggøre vores arbejde.
ExampleEnglish: The new rules will complicate our work.
ContextualTip: "Besværliggøre" is a formal, compound word made from "besvær" (trouble/hardship) and "gøre" (to make). It is used when something makes a process or action more troublesome than it needs to be.

ExampleDanish: God håndhygiejne kan forebygge sygdom.
ExampleEnglish: Good hand hygiene can prevent illness.
ContextualTip: "Forebygge" means to prevent something before it happens (proactive/preventative), whereas "forhindre" is used when actively stopping something that is already happening or about to happen.

ExampleDanish: Jeg vil overveje dit forslag i aften.
ExampleEnglish: I will consider your proposal tonight.
ContextualTip: "Overveje" is used when you are thinking deeply about a choice or decision. It is often followed by a clause starting with "om" (whether) or "at" (that).

English: to burden / to strain / to load
ExampleDanish: De tunge løft kan belaste din ryg.
ExampleEnglish: The heavy lifting can strain your back.
ContextualTip: "Belaste" can be used physically (like straining a muscle or overloading a bridge) or abstractly/financially (like burdening the environment or a budget).

ExampleDanish: Rygning kan forringe dit helbred.
ExampleEnglish: Smoking can worsen your health.
ContextualTip: Forringe is typically used when the quality, value, or condition of something becomes worse. It is often used in professional, formal, or medical contexts (e.g., forringe kvaliteten - reduce the quality). For general things getting worse, you might also hear forværre.

English: to benefit / to profit / to be of use
ExampleDanish: Det vil gavne dit helbred at dyrke mere motion.
ExampleEnglish: It will benefit your health to exercise more.
ContextualTip: Gavne is used when something has a positive effect, advantages, or is useful to someone or something. A related and very common noun is en gavn (a benefit/advantage), often seen in the phrase at være til gavn for (to be of benefit to).

ExampleDanish: Denne vej vil føre dig til stationen.
ExampleEnglish: This road will lead you to the station.
ContextualTip: Føre is a highly versatile verb. Beyond physically leading someone, it is frequently used in abstract contexts like at føre en samtale (to conduct a conversation) or at føre til (to lead to / result in).

Conjugation: går ud over / gik ud over / er gået ud over
ExampleDanish: Arbejdspresset må ikke gå ud over din nattesøvn.
ExampleEnglish: The workload must not affect your night's sleep.
ContextualTip: Gå ud over is a phrasal verb used when something negative impacts, damages, or hurts someone or something else. It often carries the nuance that one thing is suffering because of another (e.g., Det går ud over børnene - It's affecting/hurting the children). Note that the auxiliary verb is er (er gået) rather than har.

English: to restrict / to hamper / to hinder
ExampleDanish: Mangel på søvn kan hæmme din koncentration.
ExampleEnglish: Lack of sleep can hamper your concentration.
ContextualTip: Hæmme is used when something slows down, restricts, or blocks progress, growth, or development. It is often used in medical, psychological, or professional contexts (e.g., hæmme væksten - inhibit growth).

English: to remedy / to correct / to fix
ExampleDanish: Regeringen forsøger at afhjælpe boligmanglen i byen.
ExampleEnglish: The government is trying to remedy the housing shortage in the city.
ContextualTip: Afhjælpe is a formal verb used when you are resolving, mitigating, or fixing a problem, deficiency, or a negative situation (like at afhjælpe et problem or at afhjælpe mangler). Note that it is an irregular strong verb in the past tense (afhjalp).

ExampleDanish: Hun har stor erfaring inden for international handel.
ExampleEnglish: She has great experience in international trade.
ContextualTip: Erfaring is typically an uncountable noun when talking about general experience or knowledge gained over time. However, it can be pluralized (erfaringer) when referring to specific personal experiences, lessons learned, or life events. Note the correct spelling has a single 'r' in the middle, unlike the German Erfahrung.

English: to postpone / to delay / to procrastinate
ExampleDanish: Vi bliver nødt til at udskyde mødet til i morgen.
ExampleEnglish: We will have to postpone the meeting until tomorrow.
ContextualTip: Udskyde is an irregular strong verb (note the past tense udskød). It is commonly used for moving an event or a deadline to a later time. If you want to say someone is procrastinating in general, you can also use the phrase at udskyde tingene.

English: to affect / to influence / to impact
ExampleDanish: Vejret kan påvirke vores humør.
ExampleEnglish: The weather can affect our mood.
ContextualTip: Påvirke is a very common verb used for both physical and psychological influence. It can be used for positive, negative, or neutral impacts. If you want to talk about a political or social "influence" as a noun, the word is en indflydelse.

English: to see through / to figure out
ExampleDanish: Det er svært at gennemskue hans sande intentioner.
ExampleEnglish: It is difficult to see through his true intentions.
ContextualTip: Gennemskue is used when you understand a hidden motive, a complex situation, or a person's real character behind a facade. It implies a deeper level of comprehension or mental clarity rather than just seeing something physically.

ExampleDanish: De mange forskellige skilte kan nemt forvirre bilisterne.
ExampleEnglish: The many different signs can easily confuse the drivers.
ContextualTip: Forvirre is the base verb form of the adjective forvirrende that we looked at earlier. Note the spelling with a double 'r' (forvirre), which is a common small mistake to watch out for. If you want to say "I am confused", you use the past participle: Jeg er forvirret.

ExampleDanish: Det var en svær opgave, men han gennemførte den ikke desto mindre.
ExampleEnglish: It was a difficult task, but he completed it nevertheless.
ContextualTip: This expression functions as a formal adverbial phrase used to introduce a contrast, similar to "alligevel" (anyway/still), but more formal. It is often placed either at the beginning of a clause or late in the sentence for emphasis.

English: to carry out / to complete / to implement
ExampleDanish: Vi skal gennemføre projektet inden på fredag.
ExampleEnglish: We need to complete the project before Friday.
ContextualTip: This verb is often used for finishing an education, a race, or executing a planned task or political reform.

ExampleDanish: Jeg har ikke tid i dag, og jeg har heller ikke tid i morgen.
ExampleEnglish: I do not have time today, and I do not have time tomorrow either.
ContextualTip: "Heller ikke" is used to agree with a negative statement or to link two negative clauses. In main clauses, remember that the word order changes if "heller ikke" shifts position (e.g., Heller ikke jeg ved det vs. Jeg ved det heller ikke).

English: to increase / to raise / to boost
ExampleDanish: Firmaet håber på at forøge sit salg i det nye år.
ExampleEnglish: The company hopes to increase its sales in the new year.
ContextualTip: "Forøge" is often used when discussing increasing amounts, numbers, or intensity (like speed, value, or production). It is close in meaning to "øge", but "forøge" can imply making something that is already present even larger.

English: to criticize / to critique / to fault
ExampleDanish: Det er nemt at kritisere andres arbejde, men svært at gøre det bedre selv.
ExampleEnglish: It is easy to criticize other people's work, but hard to do better yourself.
ContextualTip: This verb is often followed by the preposition "for" when specifying the reason for the criticism (e.g., at kritisere nogen for noget - to criticize someone for something).

ExampleDanish: Sygdommen kan svække immunforsvaret.
ExampleEnglish: The illness can weaken the immune system.
ContextualTip: Commonly used for both physical strength and abstract concepts like trust, positions, or arguments. For the opposite meaning (to strengthen), use at styrke.

ExampleDanish: Vi skal træne regelmæssigt for at styrke kroppen.
ExampleEnglish: We must exercise regularly to strengthen the body.
ContextualTip: Commonly used for physical strength, health, or abstract concepts like relationships and the economy. It is the direct antonym of at svække.

English: to improve / to make better / to enhance
ExampleDanish: Jeg prøver at forbedre mit danske før eksamen.
ExampleEnglish: I am trying to improve my Danish before the exam.
ContextualTip: This verb is often used when talking about upgrading skills, conditions, or quality. It is a regular Group 1 (or -te) verb in its past tense form.

English: to investigate / to examine / to research
ExampleDanish: Lægen undersøgte patienten grundigt.
ExampleEnglish: The doctor examined the patient thoroughly.
ContextualTip: This verb is widely used in both everyday contexts (like a doctor examining someone or checking a document) and academic or professional contexts (like investigating a problem or researching a topic). Note that "undersøgt" is the past participle form.

English: to share / to divide / to split
ExampleDanish: Vi kan dele kagen i fire stykker.
ExampleEnglish: We can divide the cake into four pieces.
ContextualTip: "At dele" is used both for physically dividing something (like a cake) and for sharing abstract things (like an opinion, a post on social media, or an apartment with a roommate).

ExampleDanish: Du behøver ikke at vaske op i dag.
ExampleEnglish: You do not need to do the dishes today.
ContextualTip: "At behøve" is primarily used in negative sentences or with limiting words like "kun" (only). For general affirmative needs, "at have brug for" or "at skulle" is often preferred.

English: to leave / to depart / to set off
Conjugation: tager afsted / tog afsted / er taget afsted
ExampleDanish: Vi tager afsted i morgen tidlig.
ExampleEnglish: We are leaving tomorrow morning.
ContextualTip: This is a phrasal verb combination of "at tage" (to take/go) and the adverb "afsted" (away/off). Notice that the perfect tense uses "er" instead of "har" because it implies a change of place or movement.

ExampleDanish: Bilen kørte afsted i fuld fart.
ExampleEnglish: The car drove off at full speed.
ContextualTip: Often used with motion verbs like "at komme" (to get going/away) or "at skulle" (to have to leave). It implies moving toward a destination or setting off.

ExampleDanish: Det var en utrolig historie.
ExampleEnglish: It was an incredible story.
ContextualTip: When used as an adjective, it inflects based on the noun (utrolig for en-words, utroligt for et-words, and utrolige for plural/definite). When used as an adverb to mean "incredibly" or "unbelievably," it always takes the -t form, as in utroligt smukt (incredibly beautiful).

ExampleDanish: Der er gratis adgang for den offentlige sektor.
ExampleEnglish: There is free access for the public sector.
ContextualTip: This adjective shifts to offentligt with neuter nouns (et offentligt toilet) and offentlige with plural or definite nouns (de offentlige ansatte). It is also frequently used in the context of offentlig transport (public transport).

ExampleDanish: Huden er meget følsom over for sollys.
ExampleEnglish: The skin is very sensitive to sunlight.
ContextualTip: This is the common (en-word) form of the adjective. It changes to følsomt with neuter nouns (et følsomt emne) and følsomme in the plural or definite form (de følsomme oplysninger).

ExampleDanish: Hvad vil du egentlig have?
ExampleEnglish: What do you really want?
ContextualTip: As an adverb, it is incredibly common in spoken Danish to focus a question or shift a topic, functioning like "actually" or "by the way." As an adjective, it inflects to egentligt (neuter) and egentlige (plural/definite) to mean "real" or "actual."

English: to happen / to occur / to take place
ExampleDanish: Det vil aldrig ske igen.
ExampleEnglish: It will never happen again.
ContextualTip: When using "at ske" in the perfect tense, it uses the auxiliary verb "at være" (to be) instead of "at have". For example: "Det er sket" (It has happened).

ExampleDanish: Der har været en markant stigning i priserne.
ExampleEnglish: There has been a significant increase in prices.
ContextualTip: "Markant" is used to describe something that is highly noticeable, distinct, or has a strong impact. It is often used in professional or academic contexts to describe changes, differences, or improvements.

ExampleDanish: Der er en stor stigning i antallet af turister.
ExampleEnglish: There is a large increase in the number of tourists.
ContextualTip: "Stigning" comes from the verb "at stige" (to rise/climb). It is widely used in business, economics, and statistics to describe an upward trend, as well as physically describing an uphill slope or incline.

English: to consume / to use / to spend
ExampleDanish: Bilen forbruger for meget benzin.
ExampleEnglish: The car consumes too much petrol.
ContextualTip: "At forbruge" is often used in economic, environmental, or technical contexts regarding the consumption of resources, energy, or goods. The related noun is "et forbrug" (a consumption/use).

ExampleDanish: Vi skal reducere vores forbrug af plastik.
ExampleEnglish: We must reduce our consumption of plastic.
ContextualTip: "Et forbrug" is the noun form of the verb "at forbruge". It refers to the total amount of goods, energy, or resources used, and is a key term in discussions about economics, sustainability, and household budgeting.

ExampleDanish: Hun sad blandt sine venner.
ExampleEnglish: She sat among her friends.
ContextualTip: "Blandt" is used when talking about being surrounded by or part of a group of three or more people or things. It can also mean "including" or "one of" in phrases like "blandt andet" (among other things/besides).

English: to rise / to increase / to climb
ExampleDanish: Priserne fortsætter med at stige.
ExampleEnglish: Prices continue to rise.
ContextualTip: Like "at ske", this verb uses "at være" instead of "at have" when forming the perfect tense if it describes a completed change of state or movement (e.g., "prisen er steget" = "the price has risen"). It is also used in transport phrases like "at stige på" (to get on) and "at stige af" (to get off).

ExampleDanish: Han smilede, idet han gik ud af døren.
ExampleEnglish: He smiled as he went out the door.
ContextualTip: "Idet" is a versatile word that can express either time (simultaneity, meaning "at the same time as") or cause (meaning "because" or "since"). It is highly effective in written Danish to connect two actions happening at once or to provide a reason for an action.

English: to walk / to hike / to wander
ExampleDanish: De elsker at vandre i bjergene hver sommer.
ExampleEnglish: They love to hike in the mountains every summer.
ContextualTip: This verb specifically carries the connotation of walking longer distances, often in nature or scenic landscapes (like hiking or trekking), rather than just walking casually on a city sidewalk.

English: to accept / to approve / to admit
ExampleDanish: Han valgte at acceptere det nye jobtilbud.
ExampleEnglish: He chose to accept the new job offer.
ContextualTip: This word is a direct cognate to English and is used in almost all the same contexts, both for accepting physical gifts and abstract ideas or offers.

ExampleDanish: De blev nødt til at aflyse mødet.
ExampleEnglish: They had to cancel the meeting.
ContextualTip: This verb is commonly used for events, appointments, flights, or concerts. If a train or bus trip is cancelled, Danes will often use the word aflyst.

English: to replace / to relieve / to succeed
ExampleDanish: Vagten vil afløse sin kollega ved midnat.
ExampleEnglish: The guard will relieve his colleague at midnight.
ContextualTip: This word is frequently used when one person takes over a shift or duty from another (to relieve), or when a new system, trend, or person takes the place of an old one (to replace).

English: to abolish / to do away with / to eliminate
ExampleDanish: Regeringen besluttede at afskaffe den gamle lov.
ExampleEnglish: The government decided to abolish the old law.
ContextualTip: This verb is most commonly used in political, legal, or official contexts when getting rid of laws, taxes, traditions, or systems.

English: to finish / to complete / to end / to close
ExampleDanish: Hun vil afslutte sit projekt i aften.
ExampleEnglish: She wants to complete her project tonight.
ContextualTip: This verb is used when bringing something to a formal or definitive conclusion, such as finishing an education, completing a task, or closing a file on a computer.

English: to state / to indicate / to declare / to report
ExampleDanish: Du skal angive din årsindkomst på blanketten.
ExampleEnglish: You must state your annual income on the form.
ContextualTip: This verb is often used in formal, legal, or administrative contexts when you need to provide specific details, state facts, or declare items to customs. It can also mean to report or inform on someone to the authorities.

English: to concern / to relate to / to regard
ExampleDanish: Denne beslutning vil angå alle medarbejdere.
ExampleEnglish: This decision will concern all employees.
ContextualTip: This verb is often used in the phrase hvad angår (as regards / concerning) or to state that something is none of someone else's business (Det angår ikke dig).

ExampleDanish: Toget forventes at ankomme til stationen til tiden.
ExampleEnglish: The train is expected to arrive at the station on time.
ContextualTip: This verb is typically used for physical arrivals, like trains, planes, or people reaching a specific destination.

ExampleDanish: Han kan godt lide at arbejde om aftenen.
ExampleEnglish: He likes to work in the evening.
ContextualTip: This word can be both a verb (at arbejde) and a noun (et arbejde - a job/work). As a verb, it covers both regular employment and general physical or mental effort.

ExampleDanish: De vil arrangere en stor fest for hende.
ExampleEnglish: They want to organize a big party for her.
ContextualTip: This verb is used when planning events, setting up meetings, or physically arranging objects in a specific order.

English: to arrest / to detain / to apprehend
ExampleDanish: Politiet valgte at arrestere den mistænkte i nat.
ExampleEnglish: The police chose to arrest the suspect tonight.
ContextualTip: This verb is primarily used in legal and law enforcement contexts. It is a direct cognate to English, making it very straightforward to use.

ExampleDanish: Min mor kan lide at bage brød i weekenden.
ExampleEnglish: My mother likes to bake bread on the weekend.
ContextualTip: This verb is used for baking food in an oven, like bread, cakes, or cookies. It can also be used figuratively in the slang phrase at bage på nogen, which means to flirt with someone.

English: to knock / to beat / to pound
ExampleDanish: Husk at banke på døren før du går ind.
ExampleEnglish: Remember to knock on the door before you go in.
ContextualTip: This verb is most frequently used for knocking on a door, but it can also refer to a beating heart, pounding a nail with a hammer, or defeating an opponent soundly in a game.

English: to ask / to pray / to beg
ExampleDanish: Hun valgte at bede om hjælp til opgaven.
ExampleEnglish: She chose to ask for help with the task.
ContextualTip: This verb is often followed by the preposition om when it means to ask for or request something. It is also an irregular verb, changing to bad in the past tense.

English: to find oneself / to be located / to be
ExampleDanish: De fik besked på at befinde sig på kontoret klokken otte.
ExampleEnglish: They were told to find themselves at the office at eight o'clock.
ContextualTip: This verb is almost always used as a reflexive verb with sig (at befinde sig). It can refer to physical location (where you are) or to your well-being or comfort in a certain situation (at befinde sig godt means to feel good or comfortable).

ExampleDanish: Hunden forsøgte at begrave sit ben i haven.
ExampleEnglish: The dog tried to bury its bone in the garden.
ContextualTip: This verb is used both for the literal burial of a person or object in the ground, and figuratively to describe overwhelming situations, such as being buried in work (begravet i arbejde).

English: to begin / to start / to commence
ExampleDanish: Vi må begynde mødet nu.
ExampleEnglish: We must begin the meeting now.
ContextualTip: "Begynde" is often followed by the preposition "på" when starting a specific activity or project (e.g., "at begynde på et nyt arbejde" - to start a new job).

ExampleDanish: Det er en stor fejl at begå.
ExampleEnglish: It is a big mistake to commit.
ContextualTip: "At begå" is most frequently used with negative nouns, such as "en fejl" (a mistake) or "en forbrydelse" (a crime). It can also be used reflexively as "at begå sig," which means to manage, get along, or behave in a social situation.

English: to treat / to handle / to process / to discuss
ExampleDanish: Lægen ved præcis, hvordan han skal behandle patienten.
ExampleEnglish: The doctor knows exactly how to treat the patient.
ContextualTip: "At behandle" is a versatile verb. It can mean to treat a patient medically, to treat someone well or poorly in a social context, to handle materials, or to formally discuss/process a topic or case in a meeting.

ExampleDanish: Du må gerne beholde bogen.
ExampleEnglish: You are welcome to keep the book.
ContextualTip: "At beholde" means to keep possession of something you already have, whereas "at gemme" is more about hiding or saving something for later use.

ExampleDanish: Jeg må beklage, at jeg kommer for sent.
ExampleEnglish: I must apologize for being late.
ContextualTip: "At beklage" is often used in professional or formal contexts to express regret or issue an apology. It can be used transitively (e.g., "at beklage en fejl" - to regret a mistake) or as a polite phrase on its own ("Beklager!").

English: to notice / to remark / to note
ExampleDanish: Jeg glemte at bemærke, hvornår toget kørte.
ExampleEnglish: I forgot to notice when the train left.
ContextualTip: "At bemærke" can mean both to physically notice something with your senses and to make a verbal remark or comment about something.

English: to use / to utilize / to employ
ExampleDanish: Du kan benytte computeren, hvis du har brug for det.
ExampleEnglish: You can use the computer if you need to.
ContextualTip: "At benytte" is a slightly more formal or polite synonym for "at bruge." It is often used reflexively as "at benytte sig af," which means to take advantage of an opportunity or offer.

ExampleDanish: Kan du beskrive, hvordan han ser ud?
ExampleEnglish: Can you describe what he looks like?
ContextualTip: "At beskrive" can be used both for giving a factual, verbal account of something and for artistic or literary depictions.
